current-account.description: Latvia’s recorded a current account deficit widened to EUR 214 million in March 2026, compared with EUR 189 million deficit in the same month of the previous year. The goods account shortfall expanded to EUR 360 million from EUR 318 million a year earlier, while the primary income account deficit widened to EUR 107 million from EUR 32 million. On the other hand, the services account surplus increased to EUR 171 million from EUR 86 million and the secondary account surplus grew to EUR 82 million from EUR 75 million. source: Bank of Latvia
current-account.title: Latvia Current Account

gdp-per-capita-ppp.description: The Gross Domestic Product per capita in Latvia was last recorded at 38936.05 US dollars in 2024, when adjusted by purchasing power parity (PPP). The GDP per Capita, in Latvia, when adjusted by Purchasing Power Parity is equivalent to 219 percent of the world's average. source: World Bank
gdp-per-capita-ppp.title: Latvia GDP per capita PPP

gdp-per-capita.description: The Gross Domestic Product per capita in Latvia was last recorded at 16951.36 US dollars in 2024. The GDP per Capita in Latvia is equivalent to 134 percent of the world's average. GDP per Capita in Latvia averaged 10329.70 USD from 1990 until 2024, reaching an all time high of 16951.36 USD in 2024 and a record low of 4464.72 USD in 1993. source: World Bank
gdp-per-capita.title: Latvia GDP per capita

gdp-real.description: The economy of Latvia expanded by 2.9% year-on-year in the fourth quarter of 2025, accelerating from an upwardly revised 2.6% growth in the previous quarter. This marked the strongest economic expansion since Q1 2023, driven primarily by robust performances in manufacturing (6.8%), construction (10.0%), trade (2.6%), information and communication (7.4%), commercial services (6.6%), human health and social work activities (4.4%), and arts, entertainment, and recreation (3.3%). In contrast, agriculture, forestry, and fishing (-8.6%), transportation and storage (-3.5%), financial and insurance activities (-1.6%), and education (-1.2%) experienced declines. On a seasonally adjusted quarterly basis, GDP rose 0.6% in Q4, slightly easing from an upwardly revised 0.8% gain in the previous quarter. For the full year 2025, Latvia’s economy expanded by 2.1%. source: Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia
gdp-real.title: Latvia GDP Annual Growth Rate

gdp.description: The Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in Latvia was worth 43.52 billion US dollars in 2024, according to official data from the World Bank. The GDP value of Latvia represents 0.04 percent of the world economy. source: World Bank

hospital-beds.description: Hospital Beds in Latvia decreased to 4.96 per 1000 people in 2023 from 5.03 per 1000 people in 2022. Hospital Beds in Latvia averaged 8.91 per 1000 people from 1970 until 2023, reaching an all time high of 14.24 per 1000 people in 1987 and a record low of 4.96 per 1000 people in 2023. source: OECD

imports.description: Latvia's imports climbed 11.4% year-on-year to EUR 2,241.7 million in March 2026, driven by higher purchases of mineral fuels, oil and refined petroleum products (58.2%), mechanisms and mechanical devices (13.9%), and pharmaceutical products (43.5%). Imports also increased from EU countries (11.4%) and non-EU countries (16.2%). source: Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia
imports.title: Latvia Imports

industrial-production.description: Latvia’s industrial production rose 9.5% year-on-year in March 2026, following an upwardly revised 6.4% increase in the previous month. Manufacturing output rebounded to 4.4% from a 2% decline in February, driven by gains in food products (8.7% vs 3.2%), chemicals and chemical products (15.9% vs 6.5%), and recoveries in wearing apparel (3.2% vs -14.4%), wood and wood products excluding furniture, manufacture of articles of straw and plaiting materials (0.5% vs -4.9%), printing and reproduction of recorded media (1.9% vs -9.5%), and rubber and plastic products (27.7% vs -13.5%). At the same time, output in electricity, gas, and supply continued to increase (34.6% vs 33.5%), while declines in mining and quarrying eased (-21.5% vs -37.2%). On a monthly basis, industrial production rebounded to 1.3% in March from a revised 0.6% decline in February. source: Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia
industrial-production.title: Latvia Industrial Production

inflation-cpi.description: Latvia’s annual inflation rate eased to 2.9% year-on-year in April 2026 from 3.4% in the previous month, largely driven by deflation in food and non-alcoholic beverages (-0.3% vs 1.1% in March). Price growth also slowed across several categories, including alcoholic beverages and tobacco (0.6% vs 0.9%), recreation, sport, and culture (1.8% vs 3.4%), clothing and footwear (0.6% vs 2.1%), personal care, social protection, and miscellaneous goods and services (2.9% vs 6%), furnishings, household equipment, and routine household maintenance (1.2% vs 1.5%), and education (4% vs 4.6%). In contrast, inflationary pressures persisted for housing and utilities (5.1% vs 4.8%), healthcare (4.6% vs 4.1%), transport (8.5% vs 7.3%), information and communication (2.1% vs 1.4%), and restaurants and accommodation services (5.6% vs 4.2%). On a monthly basis, consumer prices slowed to 0.6% in April from 1.9% in the prior month. source: Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia
inflation-cpi.title: Latvia Inflation Rate

interest-rate.description: The benchmark interest rate in Latvia is set by the European Central Bank. terrorism-index.title: Latvia Terrorism Index

trade-balance.description: Latvia’s trade deficit widened to EUR 394.6 million in March 2026 from EUR 270.1 million in the corresponding month a year earlier. Imports climbed 11.4% year-on-year to EUR 2,241.7 million, driven by higher purchases of mineral fuels, oil and refined petroleum products (58.2%), mechanisms and mechanical devices (13.9%), and pharmaceutical products (43.5%). Imports also increased from EU countries (11.4%) and non-EU countries (16.2%). Meanwhile, exports rose at a slower pace of 6% to EUR 1,847.1 million, supported by higher shipments of mineral fuels, oil and refined petroleum products (15.6%), electrical equipment and appliances (4.6%), machinery and mechanical appliances (10%), and alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages (33.9%). Among key trading partners, exports rose to EU countries (11.5%), and CIS countries (25.8%). In the first three months of the year, the country’s trade deficit widened to EUR 900.1 million from EUR 610.3 million in the same period last year. source: Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia
trade-balance.title: Latvia Balance of Trade

unemployed-persons.description: The number of unemployed persons in Latvia decreased to 43909 in March of 2026 from 45761 in February of 2026. Unemployed Persons in Latvia averaged 86139.81 from 1993 until 2026, reaching an all time high of 194253.00 in March of 2010 and a record low of 36690.00 in January of 1993. source: State Employment Agency, Latvia
unemployed-persons.title: Latvia Unemployed Persons

unemployment.description: Latvia’s unemployment rate declined to 6.7% in the fourth quarter of 2025, down from 6.9% in the previous quarter and the same period last year. The number of unemployed fell to 63.8 thousand from 66.2 thousand in Q3, while total employment dropped to 882.8 thousand from 892.1 thousand, bringing the employment rate to 64.8%, down 0.4 percentage points quarter on quarter. Youth unemployment rose to 13.5%, up 1.4 percentage points from Q3, even as the number of unemployed young people slightly fell by 0.3 thousand to 8.5 thousand, accounting for 13.3% of all unemployed. Meanwhile, 30.6% of the population aged 15–74, or 416.8 thousand people, remained economically inactive, an increase of 6.2 thousand from the previous quarter. source: Central Statistical Bureau of Latvia
unemployment.title: Latvia Unemployment Rate

youth-unemployment-rate.description: Youth Unemployment Rate in Latvia decreased to 13.60 percent in March from 14.60 percent in February of 2026. Youth Unemployment Rate in Latvia averaged 19.18 percent from 1998 until 2026, reaching an all time high of 42.60 percent in January of 2010 and a record low of 6.90 percent in November of 2007. source: EUROSTAT
youth-unemployment-rate.title: Latvia Youth Unemployment Rate
